concepts.name: Process
concepts.description: Definition of a process as an instance of a program in execution, containing the program code, current activity represented by the program counter, processor registers, and the process stack.

concepts.name: Program vs Process
concepts.description: The distinction between a passive entity stored on disk (program) and an active entity with allocated resources loaded into primary memory (process).

concepts.name: Process State
concepts.description: The lifecycle stages of a process including New, Ready, Running, Waiting (Blocked), and Terminated, governed by operating system scheduling transitions.

concepts.name: Process Control Block
concepts.description: The data structure used by the operating system kernel to store all structural and architectural parameters of a process, including process ID, program counter, registers, and memory management profiles.

concepts.name: Context Switch
concepts.description: The kernel mechanism of saving the state of an active process or thread and restoring the execution state of another ready entity to achieve concurrency.

concepts.name: Thread
concepts.description: The smallest schedulable unit of execution within a process, sharing the process's text section, data section, and system resources while maintaining its own program counter, registers, and stack space.

concepts.name: User Thread
concepts.description: Threads managed completely above the kernel space by user-level threading libraries, executing quickly without kernel intervention but blocking the entire process if a blocking system call is made.

concepts.name: Kernel Thread
concepts.description: Threads supported and managed directly by the operating system kernel, enabling parallel execution across multi-core processors even when individual threads block.

concepts.name: Multithreading
concepts.description: Architectural models (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many) that define the relationship and mapping between user-level threads and kernel-level execution spaces.

concepts.name: Thread Scheduling
concepts.description: The mechanism by which thread libraries or the kernel allocate CPU execution time slices to independent processing threads within system execution queues.

concepts.name: FCFS Scheduling
concepts.description: First-Come, First-Served non-preemptive scheduling policy where jobs are dispatched sequentially based on arrival order, often susceptible to the convoy effect.

concepts.name: SJF Scheduling
concepts.description: Shortest Job First optimal non-preemptive scheduling protocol selecting the execution node with the lowest predicted CPU burst duration.

concepts.name: SRTF Scheduling
concepts.description: Shortest Remaining Time First scheduling model representing the preemptive variant of SJF, intercepting running processes if a shorter burst arrives.

concepts.name: Priority Scheduling
concepts.description: Algorithmic scheduling model assigning CPU ownership based on explicit internal or external numeric ranking, implemented in both preemptive and non-preemptive formats.

concepts.name: Round Robin Scheduling
concepts.description: Preemptive cyclic scheduling mechanism deploying fixed-duration processing windows (time quantums) across ready execution queues to guarantee interactive multi-user response times.

concepts.name: Turnaround Time
concepts.description: The absolute temporal measurement representing the interval spanning from resource submission to absolute process completion.

concepts.name: Waiting Time
concepts.description: The cumulative duration of temporal delays spent by a process resident inside the system ready queue awaiting CPU processing allocation.

concepts.name: Response Time
concepts.description: The precise chronological metric calculating the delta between request submission and the initiation of the first observable behavioral execution response.

concepts.name: Starvation
concepts.description: An indefinite processing layout error where low-priority tasks are perpetually denied execution opportunities due to the constant arrival of higher-ranked tasks.

concepts.name: Aging
concepts.description: A mitigation design pattern that gradually increases the priority of long-waiting, low-priority nodes inside the execution loop to prevent starvation conditions.

concepts.name: Critical Section
concepts.description: An architectural program block modifying shared system resource segments, demanding strictly isolated execution guarantees to maintain data integrity.

concepts.name: Race Condition
concepts.description: A concurrent runtime anomaly where concurrent processing threads manipulate shared variables simultaneously, making final data states completely dependent on thread execution order.

concepts.name: Mutex
concepts.description: A locking locking mechanism providing exclusive, binary resource isolation where ownership constraints dictate that the thread acquiring the lock must release it.

concepts.name: Semaphore
concepts.description: An operating system synchronization structure utilizing an internal integer metric adjusted via atomic wait() and signal() calls to manage concurrent execution paths.

concepts.name: Binary Semaphore
concepts.description: A specialized semaphore constrained strictly to integer configurations of 0 and 1, used similarly to a mutex but lacking explicit ownership verification requirements.

concepts.name: Counting Semaphore
concepts.description: A variable semaphore framework initialized to an unrestricted range size, managing thread concurrency across finite, multi-instance resource sets.

concepts.name: Monitor
concepts.description: An abstract, high-level structural synchronization paradigm encapsulating private system parameters, process functions, and condition variables to enforce single-thread class mutations natively.

concepts.name: Spin Lock
concepts.description: A baseline synchronization lock causing low-level kernel loops to evaluate access states continuously, conserving context-switch overheads at the cost of continuous processor cycles.

concepts.name: Producer Consumer Problem
concepts.description: A classical concurrent coordination paradigm ensuring data injection tasks do not run when system storage buffers are full, and extraction tasks halt when buffers are empty.

concepts.name: Readers Writers Problem
concepts.description: A fundamental multi-client synchronization challenge balancing database constraints to allow concurrent reading access while strictly requiring exclusive, isolated thread state ownership for update operations.

concepts.name: Dining Philosophers Problem
concepts.description: An algorithmic resource allocation model analyzing deadlock-avoidance criteria across highly clustered, interdependent multi-process operational layouts.

concepts.name: Deadlock
concepts.description: A severe execution bottleneck condition where a processing pool stays blocked permanently because each task retains locks on nodes wanted by companion components.

concepts.name: Necessary Conditions of Deadlock
concepts.description: The four structural runtime criteria that must be met simultaneously for a deadlock to exist: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait.

concepts.name: Resource Allocation Graph
concepts.description: A directed geometric model tracking processes and allocated resources as system vertices, where loop formations point to structural deadlocks in non-shared setups.

concepts.name: Deadlock Prevention
concepts.description: A highly restrictive structural mitigation strategy that configures the system to break at least one of the four necessary deadlock criteria before runtime initialization.

concepts.name: Deadlock Avoidance
concepts.description: A runtime tracing protocol evaluating allocation requests using current thread state vectors to ensure the system consistently stays within defined safe zones.

concepts.name: Bankers Algorithm
concepts.description: A classic avoidance testing strategy for multi-instance resource matrices that analyzes prospective task allocation steps before execution to guarantee safety vectors are maintained.

concepts.name: Deadlock Detection
concepts.description: The runtime auditing policy of using specialized tracking engines at set intervals to confirm or locate deadlock states across process allocation maps.

concepts.name: Deadlock Recovery
concepts.description: The manual or automated cleanup steps of terminating locked processes or rollbacking allocation vectors to resolve detected system blocks.

concepts.name: Logical Address
concepts.description: The virtual space reference coordinates generated natively by execution processors, requiring address map translation before referencing physical memory nodes.

concepts.name: Physical Address
concepts.description: The hardware data coordinate addressing physical system storage cells inside the primary RAM modules.

concepts.name: Address Binding
concepts.description: The mapping mechanism translating symbolic code links to explicit logical or physical device tracks, occurring during compile, load, or execution phases.

concepts.name: Paging
concepts.description: A hardware-assisted memory management strategy partitioning programs into fixed-size virtual blocks called pages, eliminating external configuration layout gaps.

concepts.name: Page Table
concepts.description: An essential internal index architecture managed by operating system contexts mapping process virtual page numbers to physical RAM page frames.

concepts.name: Segmentation
concepts.description: A logical, user-centric memory mapping schema grouping resource data allocations into variable-length structures called segments based on runtime code categories.

concepts.name: Fragmentation
concepts.description: The degraded state of system memory where unassigned storage cells are broken up or wasted, dropping overall capacity efficiency.

concepts.name: Internal Fragmentation
concepts.description: The systemic waste occurring when allocated memory units are larger than the requested payload sizes, leaving unused bytes trapped within block sectors.

concepts.name: External Fragmentation
concepts.description: The storage anomaly where total unassigned system memory space is large enough to handle allocation requests, but its fragmented layout prevents loading sequential payloads.

concepts.name: Virtual Memory
concepts.description: An advanced storage architectural technique separating a user's logical program address maps from physical memory boundaries, enabling execution of files exceeding baseline hardware RAM limits.

concepts.name: Demand Paging
concepts.description: An optimization layout strategy where virtual memory frames are only swapped from secondary storage blocks into physical RAM channels upon direct instruction execution call.

concepts.name: Page Fault
concepts.description: A hardware trap generated when an executing process references a virtual address page frame that is not currently mapped into physical RAM layers.

concepts.name: FIFO Page Replacement
concepts.description: A baseline swapping strategy tracking allocation queues linearly, selecting the absolute oldest residency page for eviction when fault criteria occur.

concepts.name: LRU Page Replacement
concepts.description: An optimized swapping policy identifying allocation frames by checking past activity, discarding pages that have remained unreferenced for the longest time.

concepts.name: Optimal Page Replacement
concepts.description: An unachievable theoretical baseline algorithm that minimizes faults by evicting pages that will not be used for the longest period in the future, used for benchmarking runtime efficiency.

concepts.name: Beladys Anomaly
concepts.description: An architectural memory anomaly seen under certain layout frameworks (like FIFO) where growing physical page frame capacities counterintuitively triggers higher page fault counts.

concepts.name: Thrashing
concepts.description: A highly critical system failure state where an operating system spends more operational time swapping pages in and out of virtual storage than performing actual instructions.

concepts.name: Network
concepts.description: A system of interconnected autonomous computing devices linked via communication links to share resources, exchange data, and enable digital communications.

concepts.name: LAN
concepts.description: Local Area Network; a network infrastructure providing data communication capabilities across highly confined geographical areas like residential buildings, schools, or individual laboratory facilities.

concepts.name: MAN
concepts.description: Metropolitan Area Network; a regional data infrastructure bridging digital elements across a town or city geographic space, spanning scales larger than individual corporate campuses.

concepts.name: WAN
concepts.description: Wide Area Network; an expansive network topology linking disparate computing endpoints across countries or continents, often using long-haul public telecommunication systems.

concepts.name: Bandwidth
concepts.description: The maximum data transmission capability of a communication link, measuring total bit delivery potential per unit time across an active data link.

concepts.name: Latency
concepts.description: The cumulative temporal delay measuring the exact time duration required for a specific data block or packet to travel from its initial origin source to its destination point.

concepts.name: Throughput
concepts.description: The actual amount of valid data successfully transmitted over a network channel within a given timeframe, reflecting real-world performance under overhead constraints.

concepts.name: Packet
concepts.description: The formatted, byte-bounded routing unit generated at the Network Layer (Layer 3), combining user data payloads with control headers containing network address metrics.

concepts.name: Frame
concepts.description: The explicit data payload unit managed within Data Link Layer boundaries (Layer 2), encapsulating packets with specialized physical synchronization headers and error detection flags.

concepts.name: Physical Layer
concepts.description: Layer 1 of the OSI stack, managing raw bitstream transmissions, mechanical connector specifications, and physical signal voltage standards over transmission media.

concepts.name: Data Link Layer
concepts.description: Layer 2 of the OSI stack, providing robust node-to-node frame encapsulation, handling error control check flags, and establishing physical MAC tracking paths.

concepts.name: Network Layer
concepts.description: Layer 3 of the OSI stack, establishing routing algorithms, virtual multi-segment connectivity pathways, and logical IP configuration schemes.

concepts.name: Transport Layer
concepts.description: Layer 4 of the OSI stack, providing reliable end-to-end data delivery, error correction mechanisms, flow control, and port-based process multiplexing.

concepts.name: Session Layer
concepts.description: Layer 5 of the OSI stack, managing, authenticating, and synchronizing long-duration application context connections across multi-system terminals.

concepts.name: Presentation Layer
concepts.description: Layer 6 of the OSI stack, translating disparate system syntax conventions via data compression, formatting adjustments, and programmatic cryptographic encryption steps.

concepts.name: Application Layer
concepts.description: Layer 7 of the OSI stack, interacting directly with user-level software applications via standard communication protocols to enable end-user network services.

concepts.name: TCP IP Architecture
concepts.description: A production-oriented four-layer networking model built on the Internet protocol suite, focusing on interoperability over strict theoretical layer isolation.

concepts.name: Application Layer
concepts.description: The top TCP/IP architectural tier combining application, presentation, and session services to directly manage interface operations.

concepts.name: Transport Layer
concepts.description: The TCP/IP execution plane responsible for end-to-end host communication management, utilizing TCP and UDP protocol engines to manage data sessions.

concepts.name: Internet Layer
concepts.description: The core TCP/IP protocol routing layout layer, anchoring host packet delivery operations across independent node tracking spaces via IP.

concepts.name: Network Access Layer
concepts.description: The foundation layer of the TCP/IP model, mapping physical hardware tracking media and link layer mechanics to connect directly with physical transmission media.

concepts.name: TCP
concepts.description: Transmission Control Protocol; a connection-oriented transport protocol providing reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of a stream of octets between applications.

concepts.name: UDP
concepts.description: User Datagram Protocol; a lightweight, connectionless transport protocol providing minimal message delivery services with no reliability guarantees, optimizing transmission speed.

concepts.name: TCP Three Way Handshake
concepts.description: The multi-step socket sync pattern (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) used to reliably establish connection parameters between client and server terminals before data transfer begins.

concepts.name: TCP Four Way Termination
concepts.description: The systematic teardown sequence (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) cleanly closing independent data lines when active communication sessions end.

concepts.name: Flow Control
concepts.description: The transport mechanism mapping sender throughput speeds to match receiver buffer capacities, preventing data overruns on slower target terminals.

concepts.name: Congestion Control
concepts.description: The network routing policy using window adjustments (Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance) to lower source output when mid-path packet buffers overload.

concepts.name: Sliding Window Protocol
concepts.description: A dynamic packet tracking framework enabling concurrent multi-segment frame transmissions before requiring explicitly returned acknowledgement markers.

concepts.name: HTTP
concepts.description: Hypertext Transfer Protocol; an application-layer protocol for distributed, collaborative, hypermedia information systems, functioning as the foundation of data communication for the World Wide Web.

concepts.name: HTTPS
concepts.description: The secure extension of HTTP, encrypting message data streams using underlying TLS/SSL cryptographic connection wrappers.

concepts.name: DNS
concepts.description: Domain Name System; a hierarchical distributed naming system mapping human-readable domain text labels to machine-routable logical IP targets.

concepts.name: DHCP
concepts.description: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol; an infrastructure utility dynamically assigning runtime network profiles, subnet masks, and gateway points to connecting devices.

concepts.name: SMTP
concepts.description: Simple Mail Transfer Protocol; the email delivery framework handling messaging transfers between source platforms and destination mail servers.

concepts.name: POP3
concepts.description: Post Office Protocol version 3; a simple mail retrieval engine pulling server inbox contents directly to a local client device, often purging original copies from the server.

concepts.name: IMAP
concepts.description: Internet Message Access Protocol; an advanced mail storage protocol allowing multiple devices to concurrently view, organize, and sync inbox content states on remote mail servers.

concepts.name: FTP
concepts.description: File Transfer Protocol; a legacy utility built on a dual-channel design pattern to handle file uploads and downloads across remote host nodes.

concepts.name: SSH
concepts.description: Secure Shell; a cryptographic protocol enabling secure command execution and remote administration over unencrypted connection networks.

concepts.name: IPv4
concepts.description: A 32-bit logical addressing schema organizing host target coordinates into four 8-bit octet segments, yielding a maximum space of around 4.3 billion unique identifiers.

concepts.name: IPv6
concepts.description: An advanced 128-bit hexadecimal addressing format developed to replace IPv4, scaling address space availability to match growing global IoT numbers.

concepts.name: Subnetting
concepts.description: The network optimization process dividing a large block of IP addresses into smaller, isolated sub-networks to improve routing efficiency and security.

concepts.name: CIDR
concepts.description: Classless Inter-Domain Routing; a flexible method for allocating IP addresses and routing packets, replacing traditional network class limits with bit-mask suffixes.

concepts.name: Router
concepts.description: An intelligent Layer 3 network appliance analyzing packet headers and route matrices to forward data across disparate network topologies.

concepts.name: Switch
concepts.description: A Layer 2 hardware appliance inspecting hardware MAC address targets to forward data frames directly to specific local port connections.

concepts.name: MAC Address
concepts.description: Media Access Control; a unique 48-bit physical identifier permanently burned into hardware network interfaces by manufacturers to handle local link navigation.

concepts.name: ARP
concepts.description: Address Resolution Protocol; a discovery protocol mapping dynamic Layer 3 logical IP addresses to specific Layer 2 physical hardware MAC coordinates within local areas.

concepts.name: NAT
concepts.description: Network Address Translation; a method mapping private, non-routable local area IP scopes into single public interfaces to conserve address availability.

concepts.name: SSL
concepts.description: Secure Sockets Layer; a legacy cryptographic framework designed to encrypt data pathways across client-server lines, now superseded by modern TLS standards.

concepts.name: TLS
concepts.description: Transport Layer Security; the current cryptographic protocol standard providing data privacy, integrity, and authentication over unsecure network layouts.

concepts.name: Digital Certificate
concepts.description: An electronic validation token signed by public Certificate Authorities verifying the identity and ownership properties of cryptographic public key nodes.

concepts.name: Firewall
concepts.description: A security system monitoring and filtering network traffic based on configured rulesets to prevent unauthorized external access.

concepts.name: VPN
concepts.description: Virtual Private Network; a secure connection overlay that encapsulates and encrypts internet traffic to safely route data streams across public lines.

concepts.name: Public Key Encryption
concepts.description: Asymmetric cryptography utilizing mathematically linked public-private key pairs, where data encrypted by the public key requires the private key to decrypt.

concepts.name: Private Key Encryption
concepts.description: Symmetric cryptography using a single shared key file for both data encryption and decryption stages, requiring secure key distribution channels.

concepts.name: Database
concepts.description: An organized, structured collection of data stored electronically, designed to support efficient data entry, retrieval, and updating operations.

concepts.name: DBMS
concepts.description: Database Management System; a comprehensive software package acting as an interface between databases and end-users to ensure structured data control.

concepts.name: RDBMS
concepts.description: Relational Database Management System; a database paradigm structuring records into strict tabular rows and columns, managing associations through explicit keys.

concepts.name: Schema
concepts.description: The complete logical configuration structure defining database rules, field relations, constraints, and record organizational data frameworks.

concepts.name: Instance
concepts.description: The actual physical snapshot capturing database values and records at a single specific chronological moment.

concepts.name: Data Independence
concepts.description: The database isolation property allowing schema adjustments at one abstraction tier (such as logical structure) without requiring modifications to other levels (like physical storage configurations).

concepts.name: Primary Key
concepts.description: A unique, non-null database index explicitly chosen to identify individual tabular record entries uniquely.

concepts.name: Foreign Key
concepts.description: A relational field constraint mapping to a primary key in another table, enforcing referential integrity across database layouts.

concepts.name: Candidate Key
concepts.description: A minimal set of attributes that can uniquely identify a table row without containing redundant tracking data.

concepts.name: Super Key
concepts.description: Any combination of table attributes that uniquely identifies a row, potentially containing extra non-essential attributes beyond candidate key limits.

concepts.name: Alternate Key
concepts.description: Candidate keys that were not selected as the primary table identifier but still provide valid, unique record identification indexes.

concepts.name: Unique Constraint
concepts.description: An indexing constraint preventing duplicate attribute entry values across a table column, while still allowing null values.

concepts.name: Not Null Constraint
concepts.description: A validation constraint enforcing that a table column cannot accept blank or null entries during record insertion operations.

concepts.name: SELECT
concepts.description: The primary data manipulation operator used to fetch specific record rows and data values from target database tables.

concepts.name: WHERE
concepts.description: A row filtering clause filtering dataset outputs based on explicit conditional logic before row evaluation grouping steps occur.

concepts.name: GROUP BY
concepts.description: An aggregation directive partitioning rows with identical values into summarized sub-groups for functional analysis calculations.

concepts.name: HAVING
concepts.description: A filtering conditional clause applying explicit selection restrictions to post-aggregation datasets generated by GROUP BY actions.

concepts.name: ORDER BY
concepts.description: A presentation directive sorting output datasets in ascending or descending order based on designated table columns.

concepts.name: Aggregate Functions
concepts.description: Mathematical calculation operations (SUM, COUNT, AVG, MIN, MAX) that process multi-row array sets to return single, consolidated summary metrics.

concepts.name: Subquery
concepts.description: An isolated nested query execution embedded inside the filtering statements of a parent SQL query block.

concepts.name: View
concepts.description: A virtual table configuration mapping a stored SQL query definition onto dynamic, real-time table projections.

concepts.name: Inner Join
concepts.description: A row evaluation join returning only the intersection matches where keys match across both source tables.

concepts.name: Left Join
concepts.description: A join returning all rows from the left source table alongside matching values from the right table, substituting nulls if no intersection is found.

concepts.name: Right Join
concepts.description: A join matching right-side database row references against corresponding left-side entries, outputting null fields for unmapped left rows.

concepts.name: Full Join
concepts.description: A broad relational link yielding all records from both target tables, filling empty fields with null markers wherever keys do not match.

concepts.name: Self Join
concepts.description: A tabular join mechanism where a database table is joined with itself to resolve internal hierarchical relationship patterns.

concepts.name: Cross Join
concepts.description: A baseline Cartesian join pairing every row of the primary table with every row of the secondary table, maximizing total row counts.

concepts.name: Functional Dependency
concepts.description: A relational structural condition where the values of one attribute set uniquely determine the values of another attribute set within a table.

concepts.name: 1NF
concepts.description: First Normal Form; structural schema adjustments requiring columns to store only atomic values and preventing repeating group structures.

concepts.name: 2NF
concepts.description: Second Normal Form; a normalization tier meeting 1NF conditions and removing all partial dependencies on composite primary key tracks.

concepts.name: 3NF
concepts.description: Third Normal Form; schema alignment fulfilling 2NF requirements and eliminating transitive dependency links on non-prime attributes.

concepts.name: BCNF
concepts.description: Boyce-Codd Normal Form; an advanced, stricter variant of 3NF requiring that every functional dependency determinant must be a candidate key.

concepts.name: Denormalization
concepts.description: An optimization strategy intentionally introducing redundant data structures into highly normalized tables to improve complex query read velocities.

concepts.name: Transaction
concepts.description: A logical unit of work executing database adjustments as a single atomic routine that must either complete fully or leave database states unchanged.

concepts.name: ACID Properties
concepts.description: The four foundational reliability principles of transaction systems: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.

concepts.name: Commit
concepts.description: The operational command that permanently writes all staged transaction changes to disk storage, making them visible to other users.

concepts.name: Rollback
concepts.description: The recovery step undoing staged transaction changes to return data structures to their pre-execution state after runtime errors occur.

concepts.name: Concurrency Control
concepts.description: The scheduling framework managing multi-client database updates to prevent structural anomalies during simultaneous table access.

concepts.name: Locking
concepts.description: An access control design pattern isolating database rows (using shared or exclusive flags) to prevent multi-transaction conflict conditions.

concepts.name: Two Phase Locking
concepts.description: A protocol ensuring transaction serializability by dividing lock management into a growing locking phase and a subsequent shrinking release phase.

concepts.name: Index
concepts.description: A specialized pointer reference layout created to speed up data row discovery without requiring full table scans.

concepts.name: Clustered Index
concepts.description: An index determining the physical storage order of data rows within a table based on a designated key attribute, limiting tables to one clustered index.

concepts.name: Non Clustered Index
concepts.description: An index structure that separates pointer search keys from physical record layout locations, pointing to target positions via logical links.

concepts.name: B Tree
concepts.description: A self-balancing search tree layout used by database backends to maintain sorted index pointers for fast block read operations.

concepts.name: Hash Index
concepts.description: A direct lookup pointer mechanism deploying hash function calculations to achieve near-instant access for exact match queries.

concepts.name: Query Optimization
concepts.description: The internal processing mechanism that evaluates execution plans to choose the most efficient pathway for processing SQL statements.

concepts.name: Class
concepts.description: An extensible blueprint framework defining structural data properties and functional methods common to all instances created from it.

concepts.name: Object
concepts.description: A discrete runtime instance instantiated from a class definition, maintaining its own internal state allocations.

concepts.name: Method
concepts.description: A programmatic function encapsulated within class boundaries, designed to operate directly on object states.

concepts.name: Constructor
concepts.description: A specialized class initialization function called automatically to instantiate an object block and assign default properties.

concepts.name: Instance Variable
concepts.description: A data field belonging to an individual object instance, where mutations isolate values from companion object copies.

concepts.name: Static Variable
concepts.description: A class-scoped variable shared across all object instances, maintaining a single value space regardless of instantiation counts.

concepts.name: Encapsulation
concepts.description: The security design practice of wrapping object states inside protective interfaces, hiding internal access mechanics using private access modifiers.

concepts.name: Abstraction
concepts.description: The structural design process of hiding low-level implementation complexities, exposing only clean interface specifications to users.

concepts.name: Inheritance
concepts.description: The reusability mechanism allowing child classes to automatically inherit fields and functions from parent classes, establishing explicit polymorphic taxonomies.

concepts.name: Polymorphism
concepts.description: The capability of different object structures to interpret identical method calls in distinct, context-specific ways.

concepts.name: Single Inheritance
concepts.description: A baseline lineage layout where a derived sub-class extends attributes from exactly one parent super-class structure.

concepts.name: Multilevel Inheritance
concepts.description: An extended inheritance line where a class derives from a child class, creating sequential structural lineage chains.

concepts.name: Hierarchical Inheritance
concepts.description: An inheritance pattern branching out where a single parent super-class provides fundamental attributes to multiple subclass entities.

concepts.name: Multiple Inheritance
concepts.description: An inheritance structure where a subclass extends fields from more than one direct parent class, often requiring interfaces to prevent lookup ambiguity.

concepts.name: Method Overloading
concepts.description: Configuring multiple functions within a single scope with identical names but unique parameter signatures, resolved at compile-time.

concepts.name: Method Overriding
concepts.description: Redefining a parent class method inside a subclass to implement specialized runtime behavior while retaining the original method signature.

concepts.name: Compile Time Polymorphism
concepts.description: Static method resolution patterns (like function overloading or template expansion) handled entirely by compilers prior to runtime execution.

concepts.name: Run Time Polymorphism
concepts.description: Dynamic method dispatch mechanisms resolving active target calls at execution time using virtual method index frameworks.

concepts.name: Interface
concepts.description: A strict structural contract defining a set of method declarations without concrete implementations, forcing implementing classes to fulfill the behaviors.

concepts.name: Abstract Class
concepts.description: A restricted partial class configuration that cannot be instantiated directly, designed to house common abstract definition paths for subclasses.

concepts.name: Association
concepts.description: A general relationship model establishing connection tracks between two unrelated class systems without declaring explicit lifecycle dependencies.

concepts.name: Aggregation
concepts.description: A weak asymmetric association modeling 'has-a' relations where child components can exist independently of their parent containers.

concepts.name: Composition
concepts.description: A strong form of aggregation where child component lifecycles are strictly bound to their parent container's lifespan.

concepts.name: Dependency
concepts.description: A short-term execution link where a class references another class system briefly as an instantiation argument or transient execution utility.

concepts.name: SOLID Principles
concepts.description: The five foundational design tenets used to create flexible, understandable, and maintainable object-oriented software architectures.

concepts.name: Single Responsibility Principle
concepts.description: The modular guideline asserting that a software class should focus on a single core operational goal, giving it only one reason to change.

concepts.name: Open Closed Principle
concepts.description: The extensibility pattern dictating that modules should be open for functional extension but closed to structural changes of their core source files.

concepts.name: Liskov Substitution Principle
concepts.description: The inheritance guideline requiring that parent class references must be completely replaceable by subclass instances without breaking application stability.

concepts.name: Interface Segregation Principle
concepts.description: The optimization tenet advising developers to split broad interfaces into smaller, specialized interfaces so clients are not forced to depend on unused methods.

concepts.name: Dependency Inversion Principle
concepts.description: The decoupling practice forcing high-level business logic components to depend on clean abstract templates rather than volatile, low-level concrete structures.

concepts.name: Scalability
concepts.description: The capacity of an engineering infrastructure to process growing payload rates efficiently by expanding available execution resources.

concepts.name: Vertical Scaling
concepts.description: Increasing the processing throughput of a system by upgrading the physical hardware capabilities (such as CPU cores, RAM modules, or storage speed) of a single node machine.

concepts.name: Horizontal Scaling
concepts.description: Expanding system capability by integrating additional individual server nodes into a distributed processing cluster.

concepts.name: High Availability
concepts.description: An architectural goal aimed at ensuring continuous service operations and minimizing system downtime through redundant infrastructure design.

concepts.name: Fault Tolerance
concepts.description: The capacity of a system to continue executing its core service tasks smoothly even during the unexpected failure of specific hardware or software components.

concepts.name: Load Balancer
concepts.description: A dedicated reverse proxy routing incoming client request traffic across a clustered pool of backend application servers to optimize resource utilization.

concepts.name: Round Robin
concepts.description: A cyclical traffic routing pattern that distributes incoming network requests sequentially across an ordered list of backend server nodes.

concepts.name: Least Connections
concepts.description: An intelligent load balancing algorithm routing new client connections to whichever backend server is managing the lowest current active session count.

concepts.name: Reverse Proxy
concepts.description: An intermediary gateway server positioned ahead of internal networks to manage client requests, handle encryption handshakes, and isolate backend servers.

concepts.name: Cache
concepts.description: A high-speed, temporary storage layer used to hold active data subsets, reducing the need to query slower primary database storage channels.

concepts.name: Cache Hit
concepts.description: An optimistic execution state where requested application records are successfully found and fetched from the fast cache index without querying storage backends.

concepts.name: Cache Miss
concepts.description: The fallback condition where requested data keys are missing from the cache, requiring the application to fetch the values from slow primary database storage.

concepts.name: Redis
concepts.description: An open-source, in-memory key-value data store used primarily as a high-performance distributed cache engine.

concepts.name: Cache Eviction
concepts.description: The strategy used to clear data from a full cache layer (using rules like LRU or LFU) to free up space for fresh record insertions.

concepts.name: Replication
concepts.description: The practice of copying database records across multiple server instances to guarantee data redundancy and boost read scaling capabilities.

concepts.name: Master Slave Architecture
concepts.description: A relational coordination pattern where a primary master node processes write operations while multiple secondary slave nodes handle read operations.

concepts.name: Sharding
concepts.description: A horizontal partitioning technique that splits large database tables into smaller subsets distributed across completely independent database servers.

concepts.name: Partitioning
concepts.description: The practice of splitting large tables into smaller logical sub-units within a single database instance to improve query search efficiency.

concepts.name: CAP Theorem
concepts.description: A distributed systems principle stating that a system can guarantee at most two of three core properties simultaneously: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.

concepts.name: Consistency
concepts.description: The data integrity goal ensuring that all nodes within a distributed cluster view and return identical database records simultaneously.

concepts.name: Availability
concepts.description: A system design guarantee ensuring that every non-failing node returns a non-error response for incoming requests, regardless of its data state.

concepts.name: Partition Tolerance
concepts.description: The capacity of a distributed system to continue operating normally despite network communication drops or packet delays between server nodes.

concepts.name: Eventual Consistency
concepts.description: A loose consistency model ensuring that data values on distributed nodes will eventually synchronize if no fresh updates are applied to the cluster.

concepts.name: Message Queue
concepts.description: An asynchronous communication buffer used to decouple applications, passing data payloads between systems via independent queues.

concepts.name: Kafka
concepts.description: A distributed log streaming framework built to ingest, replicate, and process high-throughput event data records in real time.

concepts.name: RabbitMQ
concepts.description: An open-source message broker managing complex message routing patterns using explicit exchange configurations.

concepts.name: Producer
concepts.description: An execution process responsible for creating data messages and pushing them to asynchronous message brokers or log systems.

concepts.name: Consumer
concepts.description: An application node that polls message queues to read and process incoming data streams asynchronously.

concepts.name: Monolith
concepts.description: A software architecture where all functional domains, business logic steps, and data access layers are packed into a single unified codebase.

concepts.name: Microservice
concepts.description: An architectural pattern breaking applications down into an array of small, independently deployable services that communicate via lightweight APIs.

concepts.name: API Gateway
concepts.description: A single entry point server that accepts client requests, routes traffic to backend microservices, and manages auxiliary tasks like authentication.

concepts.name: Service Discovery
concepts.description: An infrastructure registry enabling microservices to dynamically track and resolve the changing IP coordinates of companion services within a cluster.

concepts.name: Circuit Breaker
concepts.description: A design pattern that monitors microservice request failure rates, opening the circuit to return immediate fallback errors if a service down-path fails.

concepts.name: Big O Notation
concepts.description: Mathematical notation used to describe the upper bound of an algorithm's running time, defining the worst-case asymptotic growth rate as input size approaches infinity.

concepts.name: Big Omega Notation
concepts.description: Mathematical notation defining the lower bound of an algorithm's asymptotic growth rate, describing the absolute minimum resource or time execution requirements.

concepts.name: Big Theta Notation
concepts.description: Mathematical notation providing an asymptotically tight bound, capturing both upper and lower bounds to precisely define the exact growth rate of an algorithm.

concepts.name: Best Case Complexity
concepts.description: The minimum number of execution steps or operations required by an algorithm under the most ideal configuration of input data.

concepts.name: Average Case Complexity
concepts.description: The expected number of execution steps or operations calculated across all possible permutations and variations of valid input data sets.

concepts.name: Worst Case Complexity
concepts.description: The maximum absolute limit of resource consumption or execution runtime steps an algorithm will ever require for any input configuration of size N.

concepts.name: Auxiliary Space
concepts.description: The temporary or extra memory allocations introduced directly by an algorithm's logic during execution, excluding the memory space used by the input parameters themselves.

concepts.name: Recursive Stack Space
concepts.description: The implicit memory overhead consumed on the system call stack to store active frame references, local parameters, and return addresses during nested recursive calls.

concepts.name: In Place Algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm that mutates its native input structures directly without using extensive auxiliary storage allocations, strictly adhering to an O(1) extra space limitation.

concepts.name: Recursion Tree
concepts.description: A visual and analytical tree model representing execution branches of a recursive algorithm, where nodes indicate individual execution steps and subproblem decomposition costs.

concepts.name: Master Theorem
concepts.description: An algebraic formula providing instant asymptotic solutions for divide-and-conquer recurrence relations of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n).

concepts.name: Recurrence Relation
concepts.description: A mathematical equation modeling an execution routine's runtime complexity by defining its resource cost on an input of size N in terms of its performance on smaller subproblems.

concepts.name: Traversal
concepts.description: Iterating sequentially through a block of contiguous memory cells to view or process each stored data record exactly once.

concepts.name: Insertion
concepts.description: Adding a new element at a specific index within an array, requiring down-stream element shifting if target memory slots are located mid-structure.

concepts.name: Deletion
concepts.description: Removing an element from a targeted index pointer, requiring up-stream elements to shift backward to preserve sequential data placement.

concepts.name: Searching
concepts.description: Techniques for locating targeted search keys within an array sequence, matching entries either via linear evaluation or binary division rules.

concepts.name: Rotation
concepts.description: Shifting all elements of an array cyclicly by a factor of K positions, changing indexing references relative to the original array boundaries.

concepts.name: Prefix Sum Array
concepts.description: A derived preprocessing array where each indexing slot stores the cumulative total of all source values up to that specific index point.

concepts.name: Range Sum Query
concepts.description: An optimization lookup design that uses a precomputed prefix sum array to resolve range query calculations across indices [L, R] in O(1) constant time.

concepts.name: Difference Array
concepts.description: An auxiliary tracking structure that logs boundary mutations, enabling O(1) range updates across array intervals before a final prefix sum reconstruction.

concepts.name: Fixed Size Window
concepts.description: A window technique that shifts an evaluation block of a constant size K across a data sequence to evaluate sub-interval properties without nested loop overheads.

concepts.name: Variable Size Window
concepts.description: A dynamic subarray tracking strategy that expands or contracts the left and right window bounds dynamically to discover structures matching explicit condition limits.

concepts.name: Maximum Sum Subarray
concepts.description: The challenge of identifying a continuous subarray segment within a sequence that produces the highest total sum when adding its elements.

concepts.name: Longest Subarray
concepts.description: Identifying the maximal contiguous sequence span inside an array that fulfills defined parameter configurations or value metrics.

concepts.name: Opposite Direction Pointer
concepts.description: An array iteration strategy tracking elements via independent boundary pointers that step inward toward each other from opposite ends of a sequence.

concepts.name: Same Direction Pointer
concepts.description: An array tracking strategy where both references travel in the same direction at different rates, often tracking structural boundaries or read-write targets.

concepts.name: Pair Sum
concepts.description: A search technique navigating sorted arrays using binary index steps to check if any pair of values equals a targeted target value.

concepts.name: Container With Most Water
concepts.description: A classic optimization problem resolved via inward-converging boundary pointers to maximize coordinate volume bounds based on distance and height restrictions.

concepts.name: Kadane Algorithm
concepts.description: An optimized dynamic programming approach that scans an array in a single pass to track maximum local sums, resolving the maximum subarray problem in O(N) runtime.

concepts.name: Dutch National Flag
concepts.description: A three-way sorting algorithm that groups identical elements (e.g., 0s, 1s, and 2s) into sorted segments in a single pass using three directional tracking pointers.

concepts.name: Moore Voting Algorithm
concepts.description: A streaming voting algorithm that tracking balanced cancellation counters to discover a majority element (appearing more than N/2 times) in linear O(N) time.

concepts.name: String Traversal
concepts.description: Iterating sequentially through a sequence of characters to examine, transform, or filter individual characters.

concepts.name: String Comparison
concepts.description: Comparing two character arrays sequentially to determine matching equality or character sorting precedence based on lexicographical encoding rules.

concepts.name: Substring
concepts.description: A contiguous sequence of characters extracted from within a larger parent string, preserving the original character ordering.

concepts.name: Character Frequency
concepts.description: Counting the individual occurrence metrics of characters within a text string, often tracked using localized indexing array models.

concepts.name: Naive Matching
concepts.description: A brute-force string search method checking for pattern matches by shifting and evaluating alignment arrays across every index of a text sequence.

concepts.name: KMP Algorithm
concepts.description: Knuth-Morris-Pratt algorithm; an optimized pattern search tracking precomputed longest proper prefix-suffix (LPS) arrays to skip redundant comparisons during mismatches.

concepts.name: Rabin Karp
concepts.description: A pattern matching approach utilizing rolling hash calculations to scan text intervals quickly, dropping to full string validation steps only on hash matching states.

concepts.name: Palindrome Check
concepts.description: Verifying if a sequence of characters reads identically both forward and backward using symmetric boundary tracking pointers.

concepts.name: Longest Palindrome
concepts.description: Discovering the maximal length contiguous substring partition within a target sequence that reads symmetrically forward and backward.

concepts.name: Palindrome Partitioning
concepts.description: A combinatorial challenge dividing a string into distinct substring blocks where every individual segment satisfies palindrome criteria.

concepts.name: Anagram Detection
concepts.description: Verifying if two distinct strings contain an identical composition of characters by matching their underlying frequency distribution maps.

concepts.name: Frequency Count
concepts.description: Logging string character distribution footprints inside associative hash maps to enable constant-time O(1) character lookup capabilities.

concepts.name: Duplicate Detection
concepts.description: Using lookup hash sets to quickly catch duplicate character occurrences within text streams in a single linear pass.

concepts.name: Singly Linked List
concepts.description: A linear data structure composed of discrete node blocks, where each node encapsulates a data payload and a unidirectional pointer linking to the subsequent node.

concepts.name: Doubly Linked List
concepts.description: A two-way node link architecture where each block embeds distinct forward (next) and backward (prev) pointer fields to enable bidirectional navigation.

concepts.name: Circular Linked List
concepts.description: A linked list variation where the final node connects its next pointer back to the initial head node, forming a closed loop sequence.

concepts.name: Insertion
concepts.description: Adding a node into a list structure by remapping adjacent pointer links, avoiding the element-shifting costs seen in array structures.

concepts.name: Deletion
concepts.description: Removing a node block by altering reference tracks to bypass the target node, freeing its memory slot without moving neighboring elements.

concepts.name: Reverse Linked List
concepts.description: Inverting the direction of a list by iterating through nodes and remapping each link pointer to point to its preceding node instead of its successor.

concepts.name: Middle Node
concepts.description: Locating the exact center node of a list in a single pass using a slow pointer paired with a fast pointer running at double speed.

concepts.name: Cycle Detection
concepts.description: Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm; an optimization strategy detecting loops by checking if a fast pointer overtakes and meets a slow pointer within the list.

concepts.name: Merge Two Lists
concepts.description: Combining two pre-sorted linked lists into a single continuous sorted list by tracking and linking nodes sequentially.

concepts.name: Intersection Point
concepts.description: Discovering the exact shared node block where two independent linked list branches merge into a common trailing sequence.

concepts.name: Copy Random Pointer
concepts.description: Cloning a complex linked list structure where nodes carry both standard directional pointers and unpredictable random link flags.

concepts.name: Push
concepts.description: The mutation tracking action that adds a new data element directly onto the top level of a Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) stack structure.

concepts.name: Pop
concepts.description: The retrieval step that unloads and returns the topmost element from an active stack allocation frame.

concepts.name: Peek
concepts.description: An inspection call returning the value of the topmost element on a stack without removing it from the data structure.

concepts.name: Stack Overflow
concepts.description: A runtime structure error that occurs when a push action is attempted on a stack container that has reached its maximum memory allocation limit.

concepts.name: Balanced Parenthesis
concepts.description: A validation algorithm parsing symbol streams, pushing open tokens onto a stack and popping them on closing tokens to verify enclosure matching.

concepts.name: Expression Evaluation
concepts.description: Using stacks to hold operands and operators separately, allowing step-by-step arithmetic resolution of math strings.

concepts.name: Infix To Postfix
concepts.description: The process of converting a standard algebraic expression (A+B) into a postfix expression (AB+) using operator precedence stacks.

concepts.name: Postfix Evaluation
concepts.description: Evaluating a postfix token string by caching parsed operands on a stack and popping the top two values whenever an operator is encountered.

concepts.name: Next Greater Element
concepts.description: An O(N) tracking pattern that parses arrays while storing indices on a stack to find the next larger value for each element in the sequence.

concepts.name: Previous Greater Element
concepts.description: A stack-driven lookup technique that identifies the nearest larger element located to the left of a target element within an array sequence.

concepts.name: Largest Rectangle Histogram
concepts.description: An advanced geometric optimization algorithm that tracks monotonic index bars on a stack to find the largest rectangular sub-area within a histogram layout.

concepts.name: Enqueue
concepts.description: The data entry step that appends new records onto the back of a First-In, First-Out (FIFO) data structure.

concepts.name: Dequeue
concepts.description: The removal action that clears and returns the oldest entry from the front of a FIFO queue queue.

concepts.name: Circular Queue
concepts.description: An optimized queue implementation that wraps index pointers around to the beginning of the underlying storage array when reaching buffer limits, preventing memory waste.

concepts.name: Deque
concepts.description: Double-Ended Queue; an advanced queue structure allowing enqueue and dequeue operations to occur symmetrically at both the front and back ends.

concepts.name: Priority Queue
concepts.description: An abstract queue structure where entries are extracted based on assigned priority metrics rather than their insertion order.

concepts.name: Monotonic Queue
concepts.description: A specialized queue tracking items in a strict sorted order, used to find sliding window minimum or maximum values within linear time bounds.

concepts.name: Iterative Binary Search
concepts.description: A loop-driven lookup technique that continually divides a sorted target search space in half, tracking the active boundaries via local variables.

concepts.name: Recursive Binary Search
concepts.description: A divide-and-conquer search approach that updates search space bounds by passing updated index coordinates into nested recursive method calls.

concepts.name: Search In Sorted Array
concepts.description: Finding target elements in structured sequences in logarithmic O(log N) time by checking the midpoint value and halving the remaining search field.

concepts.name: Lower Bound
concepts.description: An optimization variant discovering the minimal index index in a sorted sequence where the stored value is greater than or equal to a target value.

concepts.name: Upper Bound
concepts.description: Locating the exact minimal index location where the stored array value strictly exceeds a targeted search key value.

concepts.name: Binary Search On Answer
concepts.description: An optimization strategy that applies binary choices to a range of potential solutions, checking feasibility to find the absolute minimum or maximum valid answer.

concepts.name: Node
concepts.description: The structural building block of a tree, encapsulating data values alongside pointer connections to parent or child nodes.

concepts.name: Root
concepts.description: The unique, top-level node of a tree hierarchy from which all other node paths originate, having no parent node connections.

concepts.name: Leaf
concepts.description: Terminal tree nodes located at the bottom of branches that have no child node connections.

concepts.name: Height
concepts.description: The maximum number of edge transitions tracking downwards from a specific node to the deepest reachable leaf node.

concepts.name: Depth
concepts.description: The total number of edge links tracking upward from a specific node to the top root node.

concepts.name: Preorder
concepts.description: A depth-first search (DFS) traversal layout processing nodes in a Root-Left-Right sequence pattern.

concepts.name: Inorder
concepts.description: A DFS tracking approach visiting nodes in a Left-Root-Right sequence pattern, outputting values in sorted order when applied to a BST.

concepts.name: Postorder
concepts.description: A DFS tracking sequence processing child nodes completely before evaluating their parents, following a Left-Right-Root pattern.

concepts.name: Level Order
concepts.description: A breadth-first search (BFS) strategy that visits and processes tree nodes level-by-level from top to bottom, navigating horizontal rows sequentially.

concepts.name: Diameter
concepts.description: Calculating the longest path distance between any two leaf nodes in a tree, which may or may not pass through the root node.

concepts.name: Balanced Tree
concepts.description: A tree configuration rule where the height difference between the left and right subtrees of any node never exceeds a value of one.

concepts.name: Lowest Common Ancestor
concepts.description: Identifying the deepest shared parent node shared by two target nodes within a tree hierarchy.

concepts.name: Path Sum
concepts.description: Evaluating sequential node tracks from the root down to leaf targets to check if cumulative node values match a target sum metric.

concepts.name: Insertion
concepts.description: Adding a value into a BST while maintaining ordering rules: values smaller than a node route left, while larger values route right.

concepts.name: Deletion
concepts.description: Removing a node from a BST, which requires pointer updates based on whether the node is a leaf, has one child, or has two children.

concepts.name: Search
concepts.description: Finding values in a BST by making binary choices at each node, achieving O(log N) efficiency in balanced configurations.

concepts.name: Successor
concepts.description: Finding the node with the next larger value in a BST, located at the leftmost position of the target node's right subtree.

concepts.name: Predecessor
concepts.description: Finding the node with the next smaller value in a BST, located at the rightmost position of the target node's left subtree.

concepts.name: Min Heap
concepts.description: A complete binary tree architecture where every parent node stores a value less than or equal to its child nodes, keeping the absolute minimum value at the root.

concepts.name: Max Heap
concepts.description: A complete binary tree structure where every parent node holds a value greater than or equal to its child nodes, keeping the maximum value at the root.

concepts.name: Heapify
concepts.description: The restructuring operation that reorders binary tree elements downward or upward to restore correct heap properties after data changes.

concepts.name: Top K Elements
concepts.description: An optimization strategy that maintains a min or max heap to extract the K highest or lowest values from a stream without full data sorting operations.

concepts.name: Kth Largest Element
concepts.description: Finding the K-th element in an unsorted list by maintaining a min-heap of size K, which optimizes the search to O(N log K) time.

concepts.name: Merge K Sorted Lists
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm that uses a min-heap to pick the smallest elements from K sorted inputs, merging them into a single sorted list.

concepts.name: Hash Function
concepts.description: A mathematical function that maps variable-sized input data payloads into fixed-size integer keys used for array index lookups.

concepts.name: Collision
concepts.description: A hash table conflict condition where two distinct input keys produce identical index coordinates from a hash function.

concepts.name: Chaining
concepts.description: A collision resolution technique where each hash table slot links to a secondary chain structure, like a linked list, to house colliding entries.

concepts.name: Open Addressing
concepts.description: A collision resolution method that keeps data within the main table array by probing alternative indices (using linear, quadratic, or double hashing) when a collision occurs.

concepts.name: Frequency Map
concepts.description: An associative data structure logging data values alongside their occurrence counts to allow constant-time status checks.

concepts.name: Two Sum
concepts.description: An algorithmic lookup technique using a hash set to check for target complements, solving the pair-matching problem in a single O(N) pass.

concepts.name: Duplicate Detection
concepts.description: The practice of checking elements against an associative lookup structure during a single pass to catch duplicate records immediately.

concepts.name: Adjacency Matrix
concepts.description: A 2D mapping matrix where index cell values indicate the presence or weight of directional edge links between vertex nodes.

concepts.name: Adjacency List
concepts.description: An array of linked list nodes tracking adjacent neighbor targets for each individual vertex, optimizing space for sparse graphs.

concepts.name: Directed Graph
concepts.description: A graph structure where edges have explicit directional constraints, restricting traversal paths to specified source-to-target routes.

concepts.name: Undirected Graph
concepts.description: A graph layout where edge links are symmetric, allowing bidirectional traversal between connected vertex nodes.

concepts.name: DFS
concepts.description: Depth-First Search; a traversal strategy exploring as far along a branch as possible before backtracking, implemented using recursive frames or a stack.

concepts.name: BFS
concepts.description: Breadth-First Search; a level-by-level traversal approach that explores all immediate neighbor nodes before stepping deeper, implemented using a queue.

concepts.name: Connected Components
concepts.description: Isolated groups of nodes within a graph where any two vertices are linked by valid paths, completely separate from other sub-graphs.

concepts.name: Dijkstra Algorithm
concepts.description: A single-source shortest path algorithm that uses a greedy tracking approach to calculate optimal paths across non-negative weighted graphs.

concepts.name: Bellman Ford
concepts.description: A single-source shortest path algorithm that relaxes edges repeatedly, capable of handling negative edge weights and flagging negative cycles.

concepts.name: Floyd Warshall
concepts.description: A dynamic programming algorithm calculating the shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in an all-pairs shortest path matrix configuration.

concepts.name: Topological Sort
concepts.description: A linear ordering of vertices in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where every directed edge u -> v ensures node u appears before node v.

concepts.name: Union Find
concepts.description: A disjoint-set data structure providing near constant-time operations to group sets and check for connectivity, optimized via path compression.

concepts.name: Minimum Spanning Tree
concepts.description: A subset of edges in a connected, weighted graph that connects all vertices without loops while minimizing the total edge weight sum.

concepts.name: Kruskal Algorithm
concepts.description: An MST algorithm that sorts all edges by weight and uses a Union-Find structure to greedily add edges if they do not create loops.

concepts.name: Prim Algorithm
concepts.description: A greedy MST algorithm that grows a tree from an initial starting node, repeatedly adding the cheapest edge that connects the growing tree to a new vertex.

concepts.name: Activity Selection
concepts.description: An optimization task that selects the maximal number of non-overlapping activities by sorting jobs based on their explicit end times.

concepts.name: Fractional Knapsack
concepts.description: A selection algorithm that maximizes knapsack value by sorting items by their value-to-weight ratio, allowing fractional item selections.

concepts.name: Huffman Coding
concepts.description: A data compression technique assigning variable-length prefix codes based on character frequencies, using a binary tree structure.

concepts.name: Interval Scheduling
concepts.description: A greedy approach that sorts and coordinates overlapping timeline intervals to resolve resource assignment conflicts.

concepts.name: Subset Generation
concepts.description: A combinatorial generation strategy using recursive choices to evaluate the inclusion or exclusion of each element, finding all possible subsets.

concepts.name: Permutation Generation
concepts.description: A systematic generation strategy tracking element swaps to find all possible ordering permutations of a data set.

concepts.name: Combination Generation
concepts.description: A recursive generation technique that builds subsets of a fixed length K from a larger pool while preserving unique sequence groupings.

concepts.name: N Queen
concepts.description: A constraint-satisfaction challenge placing N chess queens on an N×N board without any mutual attacks, resolved by pruning invalid recursive paths.

concepts.name: Sudoku Solver
concepts.description: An algorithm that fills grid fields step-by-step, backtracking immediately if a row, column, or subgrid constraint is broken.

concepts.name: Rat In Maze
concepts.description: A pathfinding approach that searches for viable paths through a grid maze, using backtracking steps when hitting structural walls or dead ends.

concepts.name: Memoization
concepts.description: A top-down dynamic programming optimization that caches recursive subproblem results in a lookup structure to prevent redundant calculations.

concepts.name: Tabulation
concepts.description: A bottom-up dynamic programming strategy that solves base cases first and populates an execution table sequentially toward the final solution.

concepts.name: State Transition
concepts.description: The mathematical formula or logic defining how a current problem state is calculated from its solved constituent subproblem states.

concepts.name: Fibonacci
concepts.description: The standard linear DP baseline caching the sums of previous states to resolve historical recursive series transitions in O(N) time.

concepts.name: Climbing Stairs
concepts.description: A combinatorics problem calculating structural pathways over step boundaries using a state model identical to the Fibonacci sequence.

concepts.name: House Robber
concepts.description: An optimization problem that decides between picking or skipping non-adjacent element arrays to maximize gains without triggering constraint locks.

concepts.name: Longest Common Subsequence
concepts.description: An algorithm that builds a 2D state matrix to find the longest shared subsequence match across two independent string arrays.

concepts.name: Edit Distance
concepts.description: A string metric problem that uses 2D operations to find the minimum number of character mutations (insertions, deletions, or substitutions) needed to transform one string into another.

concepts.name: Unique Paths
concepts.description: A grid routing algorithm that accumulates path values from adjacent grid cells to find total unique routing options across a 2D matrix structure.

concepts.name: 0 1 Knapsack
concepts.description: A fundamental item-selection optimization problem using a 2D matrix layout to maximize profits under strict item weight constraints, where items cannot be divided.

concepts.name: Unbounded Knapsack
concepts.description: A knapsack variation allowing infinite re-selection of items, solved by checking current weight columns against previously calculated item values.

concepts.name: Subset Sum
concepts.description: A boolean decision model checking an array elements against target matrix values to see if any subset sum exactly matches a target total.

concepts.name: Percentage Basics
concepts.description: Fundamental understanding of parts per hundred, fraction-to-percentage conversions, and establishing baseline reference values for comparison.

concepts.name: Percentage Increase
concepts.description: Calculating the relative growth of a quantity using multiplying factors, evaluated as New Value = Original Value × (1 + x/100).

concepts.name: Percentage Decrease
concepts.description: Determining the relative reduction of a value using fractional multipliers, expressed mathematically as New Value = Original Value × (1 - x/100).

concepts.name: Successive Percentage Change
concepts.description: Analyzing compound shifts applied sequentially to a changing baseline, calculated via the effective net formula: x + y + (xy/100).

concepts.name: Percentage Comparison
concepts.description: Evaluating inverse relationships, such as finding how much more or less variable A is relative to variable B when their product remains constant.

concepts.name: Cost Price
concepts.description: The total financial outlay required to acquire or manufacture a commodity, including overhead expenses like transportation and manufacturing.

concepts.name: Selling Price
concepts.description: The final transaction value at which an item is exchanged or sold to an end consumer.

concepts.name: Profit Percentage
concepts.description: The ratio of net financial gain to the baseline cost price, normalized and expressed as a percentage: [(SP - CP) / CP] × 100.

concepts.name: Loss Percentage
concepts.description: The ratio of net financial deficit to the baseline cost price, calculated as a percentage: [(CP - SP) / CP] × 100.

concepts.name: Discount
concepts.description: The intentional price reduction offered on the catalog rate, calculated as the difference between Marked Price and Selling Price.

concepts.name: Marked Price
concepts.description: The original listed price or retail tag value of an item before applying any discounts or trade reductions.

concepts.name: Ratio
concepts.description: A mathematical comparison of two or more homogeneous quantities of the same unit, expressed as a quotient to show their relative scale.

concepts.name: Proportion
concepts.description: An algebraic equation asserting the exact equality of two distinct ratio relationships, structured as a : b :: c : d.

concepts.name: Direct Proportion
concepts.description: A linear dependency between two variable elements where a change in one causes a mathematically equivalent, scaling change in the other.

concepts.name: Inverse Proportion
concepts.description: A reciprocal relationship between two variable paths where increasing one parameter causes a scaling decrease in the other, keeping their product constant.

concepts.name: Partnership Problems
concepts.description: Calculating profit distribution shares among business entities by scaling allocations to match both capital investment size and active investment duration.

concepts.name: Simple Average
concepts.description: The baseline arithmetic mean of a data set, calculated by dividing the sum of all elements by the total count of components.

concepts.name: Weighted Average
concepts.description: An advanced mean computation where individual data values are scaled by their assigned relative importance or frequency weights before averaging.

concepts.name: Combined Average
concepts.description: Determining the net arithmetic mean of a merged group by aggregating the weighted sums of distinct constituent sub-groups.

concepts.name: Simple Interest
concepts.description: A linear financial return framework where interest accrues exclusively on the original principal sum over time, calculated using I = (P × R × T) / 100.

concepts.name: Compound Interest
concepts.description: An exponential financial return structure where accrued interest is regularly added back into the principal, causing interest to earn interest in subsequent cycles.

concepts.name: Annual Compounding
concepts.description: An interest accumulation framework where yields are computed and added to the principal balance exactly once per calendar year.

concepts.name: Half Yearly Compounding
concepts.description: An interest model where compounding happens twice a year, halving the nominal annual rate and doubling the total tracking periods.

concepts.name: Effective Interest Rate
concepts.description: The actual annualized economic return rate achieved when intra-year compounding frequencies adjust baseline nominal rates upward.

concepts.name: Individual Efficiency
concepts.description: The rate of task execution tracking the fractional volume of work a single asset completes per unit time, mathematically modeled as the reciprocal of total completion days.

concepts.name: Combined Work
concepts.description: The net performance of multiple individuals executing tasks concurrently, combining their individual rates of work linearly.

concepts.name: Work Distribution
concepts.description: Dividing wages or project roles among contributing individuals relative to their individual skill parameters and total work completed.

concepts.name: Fractional Work Method
concepts.description: An algebraic approach treating total task volume as a single complete unit (1) to systematically manage and track fractional work assignments.

concepts.name: Filling Pipes
concepts.description: Inflow conduits contributing volume to a fluid container, modeled as positive work rates in system capacity balance equations.

concepts.name: Emptying Pipes
concepts.description: Outflow channels or leak configurations removing volume from a reservoir, modeled as negative work rates in capacity equations.

concepts.name: Combined Pipe Problems
concepts.description: Analyzing fluid reservoir dynamics when multiple inflow and outflow paths run simultaneously to find total net filling or draining times.

concepts.name: Speed
concepts.description: The rate of physical spatial translation per unit time, acting as the fundamental metric in motion dynamics.

concepts.name: Distance
concepts.description: The total spatial path length traversed by a moving object during its journey.

concepts.name: Time
concepts.description: The chronological duration spent by a moving asset to travel across a given path segment.

concepts.name: Average Speed
concepts.description: The net metric calculated by dividing total traveled path distance by the total accumulated time duration of a journey, balancing variable speed changes.

concepts.name: Relative Speed
concepts.description: The computed rate of spatial translation tracking one moving body from the perspective of another moving frame of reference.

concepts.name: Opposite Direction Motion
concepts.description: An analytical layout where two objects travel toward or away from each other, requiring the addition of their individual speeds to calculate their net relative speed.

concepts.name: Same Direction Motion
concepts.description: A chasing layout configuration where objects travel in identical directions, requiring the subtraction of individual speeds to calculate net closing speed.

concepts.name: Train Crossing Pole
concepts.description: Analyzing the motion of a train passing an object of negligible width, where the total distance traveled equals the length of the train itself.

concepts.name: Train Crossing Platform
concepts.description: A motion scenario where a train traverses an extended stationary structure, meaning the total distance evaluated equals the combined length of the train and the structure.

concepts.name: Train Crossing Train
concepts.description: Evaluating dynamic multi-body relative motion tasks where two distinct train objects pass or overtake each other on parallel tracks.

concepts.name: Upstream Motion
concepts.description: Navigating a vessel against the directional flow of a fluid current, resulting in a net speed calculated as boat speed minus stream speed.

concepts.name: Downstream Motion
concepts.description: Vessel navigation aligned with the directional flow of a current, yielding a net speed calculated as the sum of boat speed and stream velocity.
